article.title: Rockefeller Immunotherapy Injected Into One Tumor Triggers Body-Wide Cancer Regression in Phase 1 Trial
article.summary: An Fc-engineered CD40 antibody injected directly into tumors achieved complete remission in two metastatic cancer patients and shrank tumors across the body, with nearly 200 patients now enrolled in expanded trials.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Researchers at Rockefeller University and Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center have reported that a redesigned immunotherapy antibody, injected directly into a single tumor, triggered cancer regression throughout the body in a phase 1 clinical trial. The drug, called 2141-V11, is an Fc-engineered CD40 agonist that achieved complete remission in two patients with metastatic cancer and tumor shrinkage in four others out of twelve patients treated, according to results [published in Cancer Cell](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/40816292/). With nearly 200 patients now enrolled across expanded phase 1 and phase 2 studies targeting bladder cancer, prostate cancer, and glioblastoma, the approach represents a significant step forward for intratumoral immunotherapy.

## What We Know

The trial (NCT04059588) enrolled 12 patients with metastatic solid tumors between January 2020 and January 2024 at Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center. The patients had breast cancer (seven), melanoma (three), or renal cell carcinoma (two) and had received a median of five prior lines of therapy, according to the [study data](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11177954/). The antibody was administered via intratumoral injection once every three weeks across four dose levels ranging from 0.7 mg to 10.0 mg.

Of ten evaluable patients, six experienced decreased tumor burden. Two patients -- one with melanoma and one with breast cancer -- achieved complete responses, meaning their cancers disappeared entirely. The median duration of response was 12 months, and the median time to response was three months, as [reported by the researchers](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11177954/).

Critically, the treatment triggered regression not only in the injected tumors but also in distant, non-injected lesions. Among 12 non-injected target lesions across all patients, four showed tumor size reduction ranging from 25 percent to 100 percent, according to the [trial results](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11177954/). This so-called abscopal effect -- where local treatment produces systemic immune activation -- has been one of the most sought-after outcomes in cancer immunotherapy.

### Engineering a Safer, Stronger Antibody

CD40 is a protein found on immune cells that, when activated, can prime the immune system to attack tumors. Previous CD40 agonist antibodies showed limited clinical success because intravenous delivery caused severe systemic toxicity, including liver damage and cytokine storms. The Rockefeller team, led by immunologist Jeffrey V. Ravetch, addressed both problems simultaneously.

The 2141-V11 antibody carries five point mutations (G237D, P238D, H268D, P271G, A330R) that enhance its binding to a specific immune receptor called FcgammaRIIB, making it roughly 10 times more effective at triggering anti-tumor immune responses in preclinical models, as [described by ScienceDaily](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/03/260315225121.htm). Delivering the antibody directly into tumors rather than into the bloodstream largely eliminated the systemic toxicity that plagued earlier CD40 drugs. All treatment-related adverse events in the trial were mild to moderate (grade 1 or 2), and the maximum tolerated dose was never reached, according to the [published results](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/40816292/).

### Tertiary Lymphoid Structures as the Key Mechanism

The study revealed that the drug's effectiveness was closely tied to the formation of tertiary lymphoid structures (TLS) inside treated tumors. These organized clusters of immune cells -- including CD8+ and CD4+ T cells surrounding follicular areas of CD20+ B cells -- essentially create miniature immune command centers within the tumor itself, as [detailed in the Cancer Cell paper](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/40816292/). The two patients who achieved complete remission both showed robust TLS formation and had high T cell clonality at baseline, suggesting that pre-existing immune readiness may predict who benefits most.

In humanized mouse models, intratumoral 2141-V11 promoted de novo TLS formation that facilitated local CD8+ T cell responses independent of lymph node priming, according to the [study](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/40816292/). This local-to-systemic cascade -- where organized immune structures inside one tumor generate body-wide anti-cancer immunity -- provides a mechanistic explanation for the abscopal effects observed in patients.

## What We Don't Know

The trial was small, with only 12 patients, and the complete response rate of 20 percent, while encouraging for a monotherapy in heavily pretreated metastatic patients, leaves the majority without a dramatic benefit. Whether combining 2141-V11 with checkpoint inhibitors like anti-PD-1 antibodies could improve response rates remains an open question. Preclinical data from the [study](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11177954/) showed that in melanoma mouse models, the abscopal effect required combination with anti-PD-1, suggesting that monotherapy alone may not be sufficient for all tumor types.

The biomarker question is also unresolved. While high baseline T cell clonality correlated with complete responses, it is unclear whether this or other markers can reliably predict which patients will benefit before treatment begins. The researchers noted that biopsies from non-responding patients showed reduced immune infiltration and absence of TLS formation, according to the [published data](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11177954/), but translating these observations into a prospective patient selection tool will require larger datasets.

Finally, the requirement for injectable tumors limits the current patient population. Metastatic cancers in deep organs that cannot be accessed by needle injection would need alternative delivery strategies.

## Analysis

The 2141-V11 results arrive at a moment when the cancer immunotherapy field is searching for approaches that go beyond checkpoint inhibitors, which benefit only a subset of patients. The trial's demonstration that Fc engineering and intratumoral delivery can convert CD40 agonism from a systemically toxic failure into a well-tolerated therapy with abscopal activity represents a meaningful proof of concept.

The expanded trial program -- with nearly 200 patients across studies in bladder cancer, prostate cancer, and glioblastoma at Memorial Sloan Kettering and Duke University, as [reported by ScienceDaily](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/03/260315225121.htm) -- will provide the statistical power needed to determine whether these early signals translate into durable benefits at scale. The glioblastoma trial is particularly noteworthy, as this aggressive brain cancer has resisted virtually all immunotherapy approaches to date.

For now, the phase 1 data establishes that injecting a single tumor with a carefully engineered antibody can, in some patients, teach the immune system to hunt and destroy cancer cells throughout the body. Whether this principle can be generalized to broader patient populations remains the central question for the trials ahead.
